intentional_test_features: Benign-vs-dangerous syncope discrimination: a young adult with a classic reflex (vasovagal) faint - prodrome (visual 'greying out', lightheadedness), a clear situational/upright trigger (prolonged standing in a warm kitchen), bystander-observed pallor, and rapid complete recovery within 30 seconds with no postictal confusion - the AI Doctor should recognise the benign reflex pattern while still actively excluding dangerous cardiac/exertional syncope

Pertinent negatives that de-risk the presentation: no exertional trigger, no palpitations, no chest pain, no sudden unheralded 'drop attack', no family history of sudden death, no tongue-biting or incontinence (arguing against seizure)

Complication layer: a witnessed fall with an occipital head strike - the AI Doctor must safety-net for an evolving intracranial injury (worsening headache, vomiting, drowsiness, confusion) even though the faint itself is benign

Telehealth limits: the neurological examination, cardiac auscultation, active-stand orthostatic vitals and palpation of the scalp haematoma need in-person confirmation; the witnessed account, prodrome and trigger are patient/bystander-reportable

Triage calibration: distinguish this from an emergency - the correct action is prompt same-day in-person assessment (for the head strike and to clinically exclude a cardiac cause), not an ambulance for a fully-recovered patient, and not routine dismissal that ignores the head injury

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-03)
